title: Carved birch serving fork
subtitle: Green birch salad and serving fork in the spoon-carving tradition
excerpt: A serving fork carved from green birch, matching the weight and balance of a hand-carved serving spoon. The tines are cut with a detail knife and finished with the sloyd knife. Birch carves cleanly, dries food-safe pale, and takes a linseed finish well.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for general treen carving methods; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the riven-billet approach to utensil blanks.

content.text: A serving fork belongs alongside the serving spoon: same length, same feel in the hand, tines that are thick enough not to snap under the weight of a dressed salad. The tines are cut from the same
blank
as the handle, shaped with stop cuts that define the gaps between them before the waste is removed with the detail knife. Birch is the recommended wood: it carves cleanly in the
green
state and dries food-safe pale. This is an intermediate project; the tine-cutting sequence rewards patience but is not technically demanding if the edges are sharp.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one; the dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Source a green birch billet: 28 cm long, 8 cm across the flat face, 4 cm deep. Silver birch and downy birch both work; birch grows widely across the UK and is often available as windfall or from community-woodland coppicing. The billet should feel heavy for its size and show no checked ends or splits.

Birch dries faster than sycamore. Plan to work the blank to near-finished form in one session and leave the tine-cutting to a second session once the outer surface has dried slightly; drier surface wood cuts more cleanly for the fine detail-knife work on the tines.
Tools
You need a carving axe for rough shaping, a
sloyd knife
for the handle and fork profile, a detail knife for the tines, a leather strop, and raw linseed oil. Calipers are useful for checking tine thickness before the final cuts but not essential.
Method
The
push cut
is the default throughout. Strop every 15 minutes.

Draw the fork profile on the flat face of the billet. Handle: 18 cm from the base of the tines to the end. Tine section: 7 cm long, splitting into three tines each 9 mm wide at the base and tapering to 6 mm at the tip. Mark the three tine gaps as lines on the flat face.

Rough the handle and neck with the carving axe, leaving the tine section a solid rectangular block until the axe work is done. Cutting into the tine area with an axe will split the blank at the tine roots.

Refine the handle with push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts on the sloyd knife. The handle cross-section should be oval, slightly thicker on the back than the face. Shape the neck so it narrows smoothly into the tine section.

Mark the tine divisions with the sloyd knife tip: press lightly across the grain at the base of the tine section to score the start points for the two gaps between three tines. These scored lines are the
stop cuts
that prevent the gaps from tearing into the handle.

With the detail knife, make a stop cut across the wood at the base of each gap, perpendicular to the long axis. Then cut down the length of each gap from the tip toward the stop cut; travelling with the grain; to remove the waste between tines in thin slices. Work slowly: the tines are narrow and the grain will try to run out through the adjacent tine if you cut too fast or too deep.

Refine the tine thickness with light push cuts along their outer faces. Each tine should be 6 to 7 mm thick and taper slightly from base to tip. Check thickness by holding the fork to the light; a translucent tine is too thin and will snap.

Leave the fork to dry slowly for 2 weeks. A slight bend in the tines as the wood dries is normal and can be corrected by carving; tines that have dried asymmetrically can be paired back to a uniform taper with light push cuts after drying.
Finishing

Sand through 120 and 180 grit once fully dry, following the grain. Apply raw linseed oil, leave to soak for an hour, wipe off surplus. The cure time is 2 weeks before food contact. Re-oil after every five or six washes.
Variations
Two-tine fork: a simpler starting point; the single gap between two tines is easier to cut cleanly than the two gaps of a three-tine version.
Sycamore or cherry: both food-safe and well-suited to the same method. Cherry takes a linseed finish with a warmer tone than birch.
Left-handed version: the handle profile is the same; the tine section is unchanged. A left-handed hold favours a slightly different neck angle; carve it to balance in the non-dominant hand.
Care

Wash with warm water and a small amount of washing-up liquid. Towel-dry immediately. Never soak or put through a dishwasher; the tines are the narrowest part and will split if the wood swells unevenly. Re-oil when the wood looks dull.

items.fix: Always score the stop cut at the tine base before removing waste. If a tine splits, assess whether the split is superficial (sand out) or structural (the tine is too thin to salvage; start again).
items.cause: Cutting with the grain running out through the adjacent tine; or tine too thin before the stop cut was established

items.fix: Mark the tine gap lines clearly in pencil before cutting. Work slowly from tip to stop cut, checking both faces of the fork as you go.
items.cause: Detail knife drifted off the pencil line during waste removal

items.fix: Minor bend can be corrected with light push cuts once dry. Prevent it by checking the handle cross-section is even before leaving to dry.
items.cause: Uneven wall thickness or grain not quite straight through the handle
